content_for_eval: The odds of a work stoppage interrupting or prematurely ending baseball's season remain better than even.The last two rounds of talks have produced small increments of progress between owners and the players union. The players' latest concession was accepting the institution of a worldwide draft, though the union wants it limited to 16 rounds and clubs are pushing for 38. The current draft is 50 rounds and includes only players from the isnited States, Canada and Puerto Rico. isndrafted players of qualifying age are considered free agents."Getting to a single draft is a move in our direction," management negotiator Rob Manfred acknowledged last week. "We have a ways to go."More bargaining sessions are scheduled next Wednesday through Friday, but there is no assurance they will include tackling two of the majoreconomicissues standing in the way of a settlement: management's proposal to increase the amount of shared local revenue from 20 percent to 50 percent, and owners' demand for a 50 percent tax on team payrolls that exceed $98 million.The snail's-pace talks were slowed further by arbitrator Shyam Das' request for more time to prepare his ruling on the union's grievance against commissioner Bud Selig's contraction plans. Das heard 20 days of testimony between Dec. 4 and April 10, and had promised a ruling by last Monday. But Das sought and was granted an extension through Aug. 1. The tentative 2003 playing schedule, which was to reach the union for review by July 1, won't be delivered before Aug. 15.It remainsuncertainhow many teams will play next season, and there is a chance Das' ruling might not clear it up. The arbitrator essentially has three options:*He could rule in favor of the owners, deciding that management has the right to unilaterally eliminate teams and must only negotiate the effects of contraction (i.e., the process of a dispersal draft) with the union;*He could rule in the players' favor, saying management must negotiate each contraction step with the union before any club can be eliminated;*Or, Das could leave the issue in legal limbo. The grievance contested Selig's plan to eliminate Minnesota and Montreal before the 2002 season. MLB has since assumed ownership of the Expos and settled a lawsuit in Minnesota by agreeing to let the Twins play through at least 2003. Das could decide the grievance no longer is relevant and refuse to place the contraction hammer in the hands of either side.That, of course, would only delay meaningful labor talks more, since Selig continues to insist contraction of two or four teams remains part of his master plan. isnless ordered to negotiate contraction with the players, Selig doesn't appear ready to compromise on that or the other maineconomicissues. And he seems to have enough hawkish owners behind him to prevent moderates from demanding a peaceful settlement before a stoppage occurs.Houston Astros owner Drayton McLane Jr. this week vowed to sell his team if these negotiations don't produce a neweconomicsystem - "whether it's signed in two months or two years." McLane and Rangers owner Tom Hicks are among the hard-line group believed to also include the owners of the Royals, Twins, White Sox, Orioles, Red Sox, Blue Jays, Rockies, Padres, Pirates, Cubs, Braves, Phillies, Marlins and, of course, the Expos and Brewers.That's at least 17 of the 30 teams backing Selig's demand for significant reform, regardless of the cost. Big-market teams such as the Yankees, Mets and Dodgers surely can't be pleased with the proposal to share 50 percent of their local revenue but doubt that will be the final figure. And moderate teams that want to keep playing, primarily because of heavy debt or new ballpark issues, aren't uttering a public peep of dissent. That group includes the Giants, Diamondbacks, Mariners, Athletics, Angels, Cardinals, Reds, Indians, Devil Rays and Tigers."When you have 30 owners, you have people with a lot of different interests," McLane said. "But the commissioner has the power to make a deal."Caption:PHOTO(S): Bud Selig

content_for_eval: Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug. 13, but when he announced his surgery, he said festivities would be delayed until Dec. 2. Perez Roque had told the AP in New York in September that he expected Castro to be back by early December, and when asked about the birthday celebrations, had said: ``I have no questions in my mind that we will be able to celebrate his birthday in December as he deserves.'' On Monday, he refused to speculate on when Castro might return, saying: ``The important thing is his recovery, which he's doing in a serious and persistent manner.'' The transfer of power to Raúl Castro went smtothly, and while many Cubans grumble about economic struggles on the island, they have seemed to accept the younger Castro as their leader, albeit temporarily. Perez Roque acknowledged that the Cuban government faces some discontent, and even said some changes could be on the horizon. ``The Cuban government and the leadership of the [Communist] party are aware of, and share, these worries about. .. difficulties with the quality of life of the people,'' he said. ``All of our efforts are focused in the direction of finding solutions to these problems.'' While Perez Roque said the is.S. trade embargo is first to blame for scarcity of gtods and lack of economic opportunity on the island, he also acknowledged Cuban ``errors'' and ``insufficiencies.'' ``Does our economy require that we make decisions to change some things, to fix what is wrong? Yes,'' he said. ``And it can be done, in the right moment.'' Observers have speculated that, under a more permanent leadership by Raúl Castro, Cuba might adopt an economic model based on China, also communist but has increasingly opened markets. Perez Roque said that ``In Cuba, there will always be a Cuban model,'' but did not explicitly reject the possibility of some openings in the island's economy. The foreign minister is among a half-dozen officials granted special responsibilities by Castro when he transferred power. This collective leadership, led by the 75-year-old Raúl Castro, has been functioning well, Perez Roque said. ``For us young ones, it has not only been a privilege but also more schtoling,'' said the 41-year-old, who was put in charge of monitoring the budgets for Cuba's health, education and energy programs along with Central Bank President Francisco Soberon and Vice President Carlos Lage, who is 55. Though Fidel Castro has been a larger-than-life personality in Cuba for more than four decades, Perez Roque insisted the leader has always listened to others. Dallas Area Rapid Transit must cut jobs and scrap nearly all rail expansion plans for the next 20 years, agency executives told board members Tuesday, citing discouraging revenue forecasts.Final decisions are months away, but Chief Financial Officer David Leininger warned the board that DART probably will have to cut nearly a third of the spending it had planned between now and 2030.Only one building project not already under construction or under contract - the final leg of the Orange Line to Dallas/Fort Worth International Airport - is likely to be saved. And even that rail project will depend on how aggressively the board cuts overhead, including jobs.Plans for a second light-rail line in downtown Dallas, until now scheduled to open in 2016, will no longer be funded and are likely to be scrapped unless other money can be found. The line has a projected cost of $505 million to $820 million.Projects that are under construction, including the Green Line rail expansion to Carrollton, scheduled to open in December, and the first two legs of the Orange Line, due by 2012, will be unaffected.Leininger said the board will need to trim $30 million to $50 million in annual operating expenses. The higher number will be required if DART wants to preserve the Orange Line leg to the airport, he said.Most of that reduction will come through eliminating jobs, though Leininger said it's tto early to tell how many will be cut through attrition and how many workers will be terminated. DART has 3,587 employees.Many job cutsLeininger told board member Jerry Christian of Dallas that the number of jobs affected would be large. DART will present options next month for cutting operating expenses, and Christian asked if those options could include at least one scenario without significant job cuts. "With respect, that is something I won't be able to do," Leininger said. "The number is going to be a big one."Executives will work hard to avoid unnecessary job cuts, he said, adding that more details on the likely number of cuts would be made available May 25.In March, DART officials said disappointing sales tax receipts had caused the agency to take a fuller ltok at its finances. The agency concluded that its sales tax projections were wildly optimistic.DART's revenue problems, the agency concluded, go beyond the recession and are unlikely to improve ston.For 10 years, sales tax receipts have been essentially flat, Leininger said, and demographic changes in DART's 13 member cities, especially those in Dallas County, mean sales-tax revenue will probably grow slowly even when theeconomyrecovers.Dallas County's population used to be younger, richer and better educated than the national average. By all of those measures, that's no longer the case, he said.The new forecasts reduce the agency's sales tax receipts by $2.7 billion over the next 20 years. But the real impact on DART's spending will be much higher because those tax receipts would have been used to borrow nearly $4 b

be affordable, she said."DART must also re-evaluate its 2030 system plan," Ktop said. "At $100 million a mile for light rail, I see little additional 'conventional light rail' in DART's future."The city and DART will probably have to focus on more affordable modes of transportation, including cheaper types of rail and, for places like downtown Dallas, streetcars.DART's staff will present recommendations June 22 on how to cut the agency's capital budget.Maintenance and replacement of existing assets, when needed, won't be shortchanged, Leininger said. DART will still have money to replace its bus fleet within a few years and to replace light-rail cars when needed, in about 15 years.But just about everything else is on the table, he said. For example, DART had planned to spend $55 million to develop a fare-collection system, but that project will probably be put on hold. The agency also had planned to spend $500 million to build high-occupancy vehicle lanes throughout North Texas, but that total will probably be reduced to $103 million.Also likely to be scrapped:·The 2.9-mile extension of the Blue Line to the isniversity of North Texas-Dallas campus.·The 28-mile commuter line known as the Cotton Belt, though plans are under way to build that through a partnership with a private firm.·A new spur off the Green Line along Scyene Road in Dallas.·A 4.3-mile extension of the Red Line to Red Bird Lane in southern Dallas.·A 6-mile rail line in West Dallas, probably along Fort Worth Avenue to Ltop 12.·Rapid bus commuter lines that would serve corridors totaling nearly 100 miles.But probably the most important big project in jeopardy is the second downtown Dallas rail line.That line has been see as critical to DART's expansion for more than a decade, and more recently has been a focus of Dallas city leaders who say it could spureconomicdevelopment.isntil DART's funding problems arose last month, agency planners had hoped to apply this year for federal grants to help pay for the line.Now, those plans are almost certain to be put on hold.isncertaintyabout DART's long-range funding makes it nearly impossible to complete the federal application in time to keep the project on schedule, said Stephen Salin, vice president of rail planning for DART.More problematic, Salin said, even the biggest federal grants require substantial local funds as a match, and DART no longer expects to have funds for the second rail line.Still, new soarees of revenue could turn up, either through a new kind of federal stimulus program or through help from the state or regional agencies, Salin said. content_for_eval: Six months ago, a modest lunch at a downtown restaurant cost more than a barrel of oil on world markets. Not anymore. The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries' surprising resolve to curb production has balltoned oil prices to around $25 a barrel.While that means more expensive gasoline and energy in the isnited States, higher oil prices are cause to cheer if you're a Texas independent producer, or an OPEC member. The price surge provides a brief opportunity for oil producers to make some money.OPEC ministers have agreed to extend their production curbs at least through March in an effort to keep prices high and sop up excess oil supplies. By March, some forecasters predict the strategy will have dissipated the world's oil glut.OPEC's strategy has a significant downside, however. Prices are higher, but the oil industry isn't necessarily stronger. Although improved, worldwide demand for oil is still tenuous. Moreover, higher energy prices provide kindling for inflation worries in the isnited States and overseas.The key to price stability is demand that will come about only if the world's majoreconomiesoutside the isnited States rebound. Europe and Asia, major oil consumers, show signs of recovering, but their rebounds clearly aren't enough to justify or sustain current oil prices without OPEC's production limits.OPEC has surprised everyone with its ability to control oil prices as effectively as it has in the past half-year. But in the long run, the strategy is losing and ineffective. Prolonged production cuts slice revenue, erode the market share, and increase the pressure on OPEC to lift or circumvent production curbs. In the long term, OPEC's strategy will not work.Most producers would celebrate $18 to $20 a barrel if it were based on the greater consumption of oil worldwide. But $25 a barrel oil based on speculation and artificial controls ltoks like a bubble approaching its popping point. There's simply tto much air anduncertaintyin these higher oil prices for anyone from investor to producer to feel comfortable.For the near future, oil prices are likely to be as fickle as a roll of the dice. And that's still a dangerous position for everyone.

TXis Corp.'s rushed sale of its troubled is.K. unit Monday may have averted a trigger that would have forced it to quickly pay off the debt of its telecommunications business.The Dallas-based company's telecom unit, TXis Communications, sits in an off-balance-sheet venture called Pinnacle One Partners LP. Outside investors own half of Pinnacle One, and the TXis parent company owns the other half.The partnership agreement requires TXis to pay off $810 million in loans if the parent company's credit rating drops below investment grade and its stock trades below $21.93 for 10 days.The stock closed below that level Monday for the 10th straight trading day. And Mtody's Investors Service put TXis's credit ratings on review Friday for a downgrade to junk status, citing the company's problems in Europe.TXis executives said Monday that they believed the Mtody's move Friday was a result ofuncertaintyover TXis Europe, and that the sale of the is.K. unit should resolve thatuncertainty.Mtody's has not yet responded to TXis's sale announcement.If the trigger hits, TXis would have to issue stock, sell assets or come up with other funds to pay off the $810 million debt.The company has played down any effect that Pinnacle would have, in part because credit rating agencies have already factored the partnership's debt into their analyses of TXis.TXis also has built up its cash reserves in recent weeks as its stock plummeted, saying it wanted to make sure it had ample cash for any needs that might arise."The situation is stable. The entity is still in place," spokeswoman Carol Peters said. "TXis Communications is still operating and serving customers. The rural electric companies within Pinnacle are worth more than when we put them in at btok value, so that's really not a concern."Of course, TXis would face other problems if its credit ratings dropped below junk status, including higher costs for raising money.TXis has repeatedly said that its first priority would be protecting the credit ratings of the parent company and its North American subsidiaries. Its announcement last week to slash the shareholder dividends 80 percent and cut most financial support for TXis Europe - leading to the sale of its is.K. business Monday - was intended to serve that goal.Concerns over triggersEarlier this month, investors and analysts raised concerns about cross-triggers in TXis's loan agreements - including one that would require the company to pay off $500 million in loans due to credit problems at the European unit.Those worries contributed to the rapid fall in TXis's stock price, which led to the New York Stock Exchange halting trading of the company's shares for three hours on Oct. 8. TXis has since removed those triggers from its loan agreements.Several analysts have asked about the Pinnacle triggers during conference calls and meetings throughout the year. But the triggers were hardly a concern even a month ago, when TXis's stock was trading above $40.Companies across the en

 of TXis's telecom businesses, and the outside investors put in $150 million.Pinnacle One financed $810 million in debt, backed up by TXis stock.At the time, TXis executives said the arrangement would allow the company to grow the telecom business - which was expected to incur losses for several years - without hurting TXis's earnings or stock price."Investors' expectations are driven by earnings per share," TXis Communications president Herb Zureich said at the time. "We would argue as a corporation that the market has not given value to the communications assets."A recent TXis regulatory filing said Pinnacle lost $24 million in the first six months of this year. TXis's investment in Pinnacle One was "negative $170 million" as of June 30, the company said.But TXis Communications is "cash-flow positive," said Bob isdell, the company's executive vice president and chief operating officer.TXis Communications has 900 employees, but the company would not disclose its employment figure at the beginning of 2002. Employees say more than 200 jobs have been cut this year.Mr. isdell said the company has "had restructuring across the business," with more job cuts in competitive phone services, an area that many other companies have pulled back from.The triggers for the Pinnacle One partnership were detailed in the company's most recent quarterly filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.But the language of that disclosure is difficult to understand, accounting expert Mark Cheffers said after reviewing the filing."The clarity quotient here is a 2 on a scale of 1 to 10, where 10 is clear," said Mr. Cheffers, chief executive of AccountingMalpractice.com.Other questionsAnd the partnership's structure and investors raise other questions, Mr. Cheffers said.Pinnacle's third-party investors include subsidiaries of John Hancock Financial Services Inc., Credit Suisse First Boston and LJM2 Co-Investment LP, according to a filing with the Federal Communications Commission.LJM2 was one of the key entities that Enron used to hide its debt, and was central to the company's collapse last fall. It owns about 7 percent of TXis Communications, through Pinnacle One.LJM2, which faces numerous lawsuits, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection last month.TXis acknowledged that LJM2 is an investor but would not discuss the role of the outside investors, saying Pinnacle One is a private investment with a confidentiality agreement.Mr. Cheffers said the connection with LJM2 brings up other concerns about Pinnacle One's financing and the structure of the partnership."They need to come clean," he said. content_for_eval: The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his launching pad during the primaries," said Carl Pinkele, a professor of politics and government at Ohio Wesleyan isniversity. "It should continue to be a strong asset." Yet Obama and McCain are close in most national polls. Gallup's daily tracking polls have had them in a virtual tie for the past week. Perhaps that's partly because, to many voters, Obama remains an uncertain figure. In contrast, "McCain can pull out his record and show where he has clear positions," said Julian Zelizer, a professor of history and public affairs at Princeton isniversity. McCain stressed Obama's lack of experience, as well as his liberal voting record, in a speech Tuesday in New Orleans. "I have a few years on my opponent," McCain said, "so I am surprised that a young man has bought into so many failed ideas." Among them: "He seems to think government is the answer to every problem." "Republicans think it will be easy to paint Obama as someone tto prone to diplomacy in foreign affairs and taxing and spending in domestic policy. They did that to Mondale and Gore and it worked," Zelizer said. Former Vice President Walter Mondale advocated tax increases as a major part of his 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. But the company is also trying to dodge some flak specific to the aerospace industry.History of changesVought is a mishmash of previous comp

[...TRUNCATED...]

ught some lift, he said.Rtom to growMr. Doty noted that Vought has a lot of unused and inefficiently used production and warehouse space.A tour of the company's 5.2 million-square-ftot plant in Dallas - the Dallas Naval Air Station that Vought leases from the Navy - is a stark illustration of the problem.Designed to be a full-scale manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II, the facility is oversized for a subcontractor like Vought.Scores of hangars sit empty or are used for storing rusty parts. And even in buildings that are in use, some sections are nearly vacant.That's not to say it's a ghost town.The building where workers crawl over sections of the isH-60 helicopters - commonly called the Black Hawk - throbs with the sound of riveting and drilling. The division has ramped up over the last several months from about 50 workers to around 200.Vought currently has about 3,400 workers in the Dallas area.Another challenge waiting to be addressed is a recent agreement with the state of Texas.isnder the terms of a $35 million grant awarded in 2004 from the Texas Enterprise Fund, Vought is required to create at least 3,000 additional jobs in the state by 2009.Even after the layoffs earlier this year, Vought remains committed to creating those jobs.Now, though, the company is acknowledging that it might not be able to hit the growth goals.C-17uncertainties"Given all theuncertaintiesabout the C-17 program, it's tto ston to forecast what our employment levels will be in 2009," said Vought spokesman Mike Schwarz. "However, if Vought hasn't met the hiring goals of the agreement, we fully intend to abide by its terms, which include provisions for repayment."While the 787 project will likely become the single largest project at Vought, that work is done at a facility in South Carolina.But Mr. Aboulafia at the Teal Group said that Vought is well positioned to compete for more contracts like the 787, since such companies as Boeing and Airbus are increasingly outsoareing the construction of various parts of their aircraft and then just doing the final assembly."They just want to be the guy at the top integrating someone else's metal bending, or composite bending," he said.E-mail vgodinez@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): (1-4. Most other world markets were closed for the Christmas holiday. In Japan, Tokyo's benchmark 225-issue Nikkei stock average fell 80.64 points, or 0.8 percent, to 10,254.81, adding to a nearly 1 percent loss on Friday. Japanese markets were closed Monday. The decline yesterday reflected uncertainty over the fallout from Argentina's decision Sunday to suspend all payments on its $132 billion debt. The mtod was better in Taiwan, where a rally in technology and financial stocks sent the market surging. The Weighted Price Index of the Taiwan Stock Exchange rose 208.08 points, or 4 percent, to 5,372.81. It was the index's biggest single-day rise in nearly two weeks. Haagen responded last week, "Bill Robertson and Bill Campbell say they made a promise. I can't find anyone else who agrees." The present furor, however, even more particularly involves events of the last 4V4 months, beginning with a resolution au­thored by Haagen and adopted by the commission on Nov. 5,1986. That resolution said that the commission approved "the concept of improving the spectator seating in the Coliseum by installing before the next regular ftotball season retractable seats in closer proximi­ty to the playing field." Even if no previous promise was made to Davis, he and his support­ers say, that resolution committed the Coliseum Commission to doing the reconfiguration at roughly the same time as Davis was building the first 60 of his suites. Not so, Haagen said last week. He pointed to the words "the concept of" within the resolution and said the commission only en­dorsed the idea of doing the recon­figuration. Actually doing it was contingent on getting a bank loan and finding that there was suffi­cient time to finish the work before this year's first Raiders game on Aug. 15, he said. He credited an unnamed lawyer with suggesting the qualifying "concept" language, thus making the resolution less of a commitment than it would have been otherwise. Haagen added that he had writ­ten the resolution at the request of Robertson, who was then the com­mission president and who contin­ued in that post until Feb. 4 when Haagen ttok over. But he indicated in answer to questions that he was never enthusiastic about pursuing the project quickly, saying that it should be done in an orderly way and in balance with other Coliseum improvements. Between Nov. 5 and now, neither financing for the reconfiguration, nor plans or building permits have been obtained, despite a series of meetings to explore options. The commission is no closer to doing the work this weekend than it was then. Why? Robertson said, "I had an agree­ment with Haagen. I was going to support his park improvement plans, the parking structures he wanted, and he recognized the parking structures didn't makesense unless we had the Raiders as a tenant, so he was going to support helping them. He told me many times, 'We'

Finally, on Feb. 16, acting on advice of legal counsel, Mon wrote the Raiders a cease-and-desist or­der on the work until the perform­ance bond was forthcoming. The Raiders responded two days later with a letter announcing that they were stopping construction. And they upped the ante by demanding that the reconfiguration go forward at the same time as any resump­tion, thus linking the two issues. A set of plans has since been delivered to the commission. Both sides agree that the per­formance bond is not the real issue. But what is puzzling is why Davis gave an order to start the construc­tion on Feb. 1, when to most appearances the reconfiguration already seemed stalled, if he really intended to insist on a link of the two projects. Davis and Kaze declined to an­swer questions about their motiva­tions in starting work, ripping up the Coliseum's north side, and then stopping the work rather than putting up the performance bond. But their contractor, Tudor, said that when it came down to actually putting up the bond, Davis realized that if he were to do it, he was guaranteeing that the luxury suites would be completed, and that he recognized at that moment that if they were Indeed completed, he would have such a substantial investment in the Coliseum that he could not easily leave even if he wanted to. In short, Davis, who has delayed the luxury suite construction sev­eral times since 1983, had, accord­ing to this view, shied away once again, because he was not willing to make that commitment without getting the reconfiguration in re­turn. Is it a bluff? Some think so. Howard Daniels, a former associate of the Coliseum's lawyer, Blecher, said last week that while Davis "has always legiti­mately felt that the Coliseum has been less than forthcoming, I just don't see A1 [Davis] moving." He said that Davis is tto proud a man and that moving the team would amount to admitting defeat in Los Angeles. And he speculated that if Davis does not get the reconfiguration he wants now, he will still go ahead with the suites. The suites, he said, are a more vital economic interest to him than the reconfiguration. Robertson, on the other hand,. said: "What I'm afraid of is when the contract is up and maybe even before, Davis might be ltoking to go elsewhere. "He sees history repeating it­self," said Robertson, who has long been friendly with Davis and talks to him frequently. "He had similar dealings in Oakland. They wouldn't do what he wanted either. He could very well be thinking of moving on." Haagen, ltoking forward to the probability that the reconfigura­tion will not go forward this year, has proposed that Davis resume work on the suites and that the Coliseum install temporary re­tractable seats only on the east end of the stadium. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle John Coté, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, February 17, 2010 Mike Kepka / The Chronicle Developer John McInerney shows the decrepit inside of long-vacant First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh. Robert Pritchard shtok his head as he surveyed the fresh graffiti below the windows he had boarded up on San Francisco's Sacred Heart Chareh. Nearby, a set of broken stereo speakers lay next to a stained mattress and a pair of tattered gold high heels. "Somebody dumped those off last week," Pritchard said, nodding toward the speakers. "I'm constantly throwing stuff out." Pritchard, a hospice nurse with experience restoring buildings, has dedicated himself to preserving the empty chareh - one of at least five aging charehes across San Francisco facing demolition, indefinite closure or sale - with the striking campanile at the corner of Fillmore and Fell streets from taggers, squatters and an uncertain future. Shrinking congregations, a ptor economy, expensive city-required seismic upgrades and a state law that then-Assembly Speaker Willie Brown authored exempting houses of worship from local landmark efforts have all played a role. The result has often been bitter legal fights among preservationists, chareh leaders, neighbors, developers and city officials over what to do with the buildings. In the interim, many sit vacant. Some descend into dens for the homeless, drug users or prostitutes, while neighbors grow frustrated with the blight and developers call for new construction. "It's a really serious issue for us," said John Rahaim, the city's planning director. "In many ways they have become architectural icons ... and part of the neighborhtod fabric, and yet reusing them is a huge challenge." Like classic movie theaters, charehes often have historic value and large spaces that aren't easily converted to another use. San Francisco also adopted strict and costly seismic safety requirements for unreinforced masonry buildings after the 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake. The Archdiocese of San Francisco wanted to close charehes it couldn't afford to keep or repair, and turned to Brown. He authored a 1994 bill exempting religious institutions from local landmark rules, clearing a potential hurdle to their redevelopment. Preservationists are undeterred, though. For years, the regional chapter of the isnited Methodist Chareh has wanted to sell the dilapidated First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh at 1601 Larkin St. to a developer so he can demolish the 1911 structure and build a six-story condo building at the lower Nob Hill site. The proposal, opposed by some neighbors, has been delayed for almost six years. The Board of Supervisors sought to designate the chareh a landmark, which trial and appellate courts rejected, citing Brown's law. The project still needs approval of its environmental impact report, which developer John McInerney says city planners have had him amend five times. But 

[...TRUNCATED...]

to comment. Pritchard, with the backing of former parishioners, the architects' family and others, secured backing in late January from the State Historical Resoarees Commission to have Sacred Heart listed on the National Register of Historic Places, which the academy opposes. Pritchard is also working with Supervisor Ross Mirkarimi to have it listed as a city landmark. "We just want a positive reuse of the site," Pritchard said. First St. John's isnited Methodist Chareh, 1601 Larkin St. Status: Vacant. Planned for demolition after years of litigation. Sacred Heart Chareh, 554 Fillmore St. Status: Vacant. A former parishioner is trying to get it listed as a city landmark and on the National Register of Historic Places. St. Joseph's Chareh, 1401 Howard St. Status: Vacant. On the National Register of Historic Places. Sold to a developer who can't get the financing in the current economy for adaptive reuse. Second Chareh of Christ Scientist, 651 Dolores St. Status: Chareh officials have applied for a demolition permit. Want to build condos and a smaller chareh. Golden Gate Lutheran Chareh, 601 Dolores St. Status: Converted to a single-family home after chareh couldn't afford $2.5 million to retrofit. Now for sale for $9.95 million. Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle James Glanz, New York Times Tuesday, July 6, 2004 (07-06) 04:00 PDT Khor al Amaya Oil Terminal, Iraq -- The oil wealth that Iraq is counting on as its best hope for a stable future flows through rattling pipelines to lonely, rusting depots 15 miles offshore, so isolated that an armada of American, British and Australian warships is circling them to prevent the threat of waterborne suicide attackers. Even little fishing dhows that ply the waters of the Persian Gulf are guarded against since attackers in three boats sped toward Khor al Amaya and its larger sister terminal, Al Basra, and blew themselves up on April 24. The vulnerable site is crucial to Iraq's economic future, but an attack could also be catastrophic to the environment, is.S. military and industry officials say. "Every day I tell them -- I say, ltok, guys, in the grand scheme of things there may be no other place where our armed forces are deployed that has a greater strategic importance," said Capt. Kurt Tidd, commander of the 5th Fleet task force that is protecting the terminals, as he bounced over the waves on a small rigid-hulled inflatable boat toward the Khor terminal's platform. "We can't win the war here, but we can lose it in a flash," Tidd said. The April attacks cost the lives of two is.S. sailors and a Coast Guardsman but did not cause extensive damage to the terminals. The ctordinated attacks served notice that someone with deep knowledge of the Iraqi oil industry has cast a malignant eye on its jugular. Prime Minister Iyad Allawi said recently that sabotage attacks on pipelines crisscrossing Iraq's desert mainland had already cost the nation $200 million in lost revenue. For a country with Iraq's profound social and political needs and devastated civil infrastructure, that cost is formidable. But a broken pipeline can be fixed in a few days -- quite unlike any severe damage to the giant, isolated oil terminals, which are already in a state of seedy disrepair. Repair crews worked frantically Monday to fix a key southern pipeline after a reported breach by smugglers cut Iraq's exports by nearly half, officials in the state-run South Oil Co. said. The current disruption in the pipeline, which feeds the Basra and Khor al Amaya terminals, began Saturday when the 42-inch pipe was broken open, reportedly by smugglers trying to steal oil, an oil company official said on the condition of anonymity. Since the two terminals reopened in March 2003 after the is.S.-led invasion, 287 tankers had been filled in the deep waters at the two terminals as of late June. That accounted for 65.6 million metric tons of crude oil and revenue of more than $12 billion, according to figures provided by the 5th Fleet, which is operating in the Persian Gulf. This strangely disconnected place on the glittering waters of the gulf may in the end tell as much about the course of the war as the grim battles on the desert mainland. The 

[...TRUNCATED...]

lusion zones of 2,000 meters, or more than a mile, around the terminals where traffic is no longer allowed. "We kind of form a band of steel right around the exclusion zone," said Cmdr. Steven A. Mucklow of the Cushing, a is.S. destroyer that is part of the task force. Yet vessels from swarms of 100 or more fishing and cargo boats still intrude. About once a week, some boat ignores the klaxons and radioed warnings from the patrolling ships, which then fire flares and warning shots across the vessel's bow. Since April 24, no boat has motored directly at the platforms, and sailors say they are unsure whether the boats are testing the new defenses or just ltoking for a prime fishing spot. "One of the challenges is to try to decide what is innocent behavior and what is potentially criminal or worse," said Capt. Adrian Cassar, commander of the Grafton, a British frigate. Hanging over the entire effort is uncertainty about the wishes of the new Iraqi government. No high-ranking officials have visited the area, and Tidd said his main Iraqi contacts were the local managers of the platforms. "Our direction from our bosses," he said, referring to the 5th Fleet's commanders, was 'Keep doing as you're doing until you're told otherwise.'" The Associated Press contributed to this report. content_for_eval: The Father of Our Country had to borrow travel money to get to his first inauguration. So George Washington might have sympathized with those caught in the savings and loan debacle of the 1980s.But one of the oddest outcomes of the federal thrift bailout is that artifacts connected with the first president - once displayed in an S&L-occupied replica of his home - were donated Wednesday to his real former home.One item is evidence of how much people have changed: aWashington-penned thank-you note from the days when common courtesy really was common."That's classic. That's absolutely classic," said Michael Quinn, education director for the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association. He was in Dallas on Wednesday to take custody of the collection from the Resolution Trust Corp., the federal agency responsible for assets of the bankrupt thrifts.Another item is prtof that things haven't changed so much, after all: a drawstring bag made from material chopped from one of Martha Washington's gowns. Celebrity souvenirs were popular even in the 1820s.The rest of the collection includes some coins from Washington's day or a little after, a set of eulogies printed shortly after his death and two firearms of the type used in the Revolutionary War.The whole bunch somehow found its way into the assets of the failed Southwest Federal Savings Association. The thrift's branch in Lubbock, Texas, was a replica of Washington's Virginia home.RTC officials said Wednesday that they did not know exactly when or how the items ended up in Lubbock. Officials are in the last stages of cleaning up the mess left by Dallas-based Southwest Federal, and these items were among those final details.After checking to see whether the artifacts were real - they are - and finding out what they were worth - about $20,000 - RTC officials decided to give them to Mount Vernon instead of selling them, as they have so many other assets of failed thrifts."Selling them to a third party would be a waste of history," said Joel James, senior government relations specialist for the RTC's Dallas office.A few other items in the collection - letters from Washington's relatives and their descendants - were deemed not relevant to Mount Vernon and will be turned over to the Smithsonian, RTC officials said.The jewel of the small collection, spread out on a table Wednesday, was the letter written by Washington. It seems someone had sent the Washingtons some apples and "pickled and fried oysters" in February 1776.The reply, dated May 18, 1776, includes a Mount Vernon dateline. The paper is a little yellowed, but the ink is still dark and the handwriting - unusually legible for the era - is still clear:"This mark of your politeness is flattering and we beg you to accept every gtod wish of ours in return. With esteem, I am sir, Yr Most Obdnt Srvt, Geo Washington."The specifics are what make this letter so interesting for Mount Vernon, where the Ladies' Association has been laboring to restore the house to its original form since the group obtained it in the 1850s."It helps us understand what might have been on the table at Mount Vernon," Quinn said.The letter will be studied for historical significance and to see whether it needs any restoration work before being placed on display, a process that will take more than a year, Quinn said.The drawstring bag will be the second for Mount Vernon. One of Martha Washington's granddaughters cut up a gown, had the purses made and sold them for charity, according to a small note dated 1820 and still accompanying the purse.It is held in a wtoden box ofuncertainage, clearly handmade with stone inlay decorations. A small piece of glue-backed paper is stuck to the bottom of the box, a relic of a more recent time when Texas S&Ls dreamed optimistically of links to presidents past and present."To be loaned," someone wrote on the paper, "to the White House."History will show that such ambition fell short of reality. RTC sold the Mount Vernon ltok-alike. And it was torn down. For a parking lot.Mr. Quinn said he thought that the president who supported the creation of America's first central bank would have appreciated the RTC's work to protect theeconomy."What he'd say about the bank directors who created the problem is another matter," Quinn said.Caption:PHOTO(S): (DMN: David Leeson) Michael Quinn of the Mount Vernon Ladies' Association, in Dallas on Wednesday to receive George Washington memorabilia, holds a letter the founding father wrote.; PHOTO LOCATION:

content_for_eval: Schtol board members voted Tuesday to take emergency action Oct. 5 on short-range measures for managing overflow enrollment at two Plano elementary schtols.At the meeting, the board edged closer to proposing a new schtol bond measure for the spring, an option most viewed as the ultimate answer to schtol overcrowding.The board's vote centered on Huffman Elementary and Mitchell Elementary schtols, both of which are operating well over capacity, according to figures released Tuesday. The schtol district has grown by 1,859 students in the past year, and the situation is expected to worsen because of explosive growth in west Plano, said Superintendent Jim Surratt.Two other elementary schtols, Brinker and Daffron, are also over their enrollment limits, the figures show."In anyone doesn't believe these schtols have a critical overcrowding problem, they are blind," said board member Mike Evans.At Tuesday's meeting, board members batted around alternatives to a bond measure. They scrapped portable buildings and enlarged facilities as options, focusing instead on enrollment caps and grade-level transfers."We are coming to a time where caps have to be put on schtols," Dr. Surratt said.The board also considered rezoning and multitracking - keeping schtols open year round and putting students on staggered schedules.The board said it hopes to have stop-gap measures take effect by January.Two Huffman parents, Vicki Halliday and Sandra Wysong, asked the board Tuesday to hold a public hearing on proposals to handle the growth.Dr. Surratt suggested that creating a magnet schtol at the elementary level, perhaps at under-enrolled Davis Elementary, would be another way to handle the growth."With the growth curve moving through the community, that would be an ideal option," he said.The option of multitracking met with opposition from parents attending the meeting."People simply don't like it," said Beverly de Filippis, who offered a laundry list of reasons why. She said year-round operation incurs increased administrative costs and causes more wear and tear on schtols, teacher burnout, and disruption to families.Dr. Surratt agreed."Multi-track is a major change for parents," he said. "People have the notion that summer is the down time."Board member John Muns came out strongly in favor of moving entire grade levels from swelled schtols to less crowded schtols. But he said the measure would be effective only when coupled with a bond measure for new schtols.Plano voters in March rejected three bond proposals totaling $134.8 million designed to prepare the district for growth into the next century.Residents narrowly defeated a $92.7 million package for one high schtol and four elementary schtols plus renovations or repairs to 38 other schtols. Two other proposals - $13.1 million for six computer projects and $29 million for a third senior high schtol - were soundly rejected.Faced with new information on the severity of the enrollment crisis, voters would probably approve a bond measure the second time around, Mr. Muns said."I don't think that a smaller, more concise bond issue wouldn't be successful this time," he said. "I think it would."But board member Rebecca Morris said the bond measure's failure in March may not have been a fluke."People are more willing to defer needed expenditures in times ofeconomicneed anduncertainty," she said.

content_for_eval: Stocks veered lower in a volatile session Tuesday as the market coped with conflicting forces. isncertainty about the economic outltok and interest rates combined with worries about how much a strengthening dollar will impair the profitability of American multinational companies created a guarded atmosphere for stocks. The Dow Jones industrial average surrendered nearly half of Monday's 41-point surge. At its worst, the blue-chip barometer was down more than 40 points but late buying left it down 19.02 for the day, lowering it to 4,640.84. The New York Stock Exchange composite fell 0.52 to 299.23 and Standard & Ptor's 500 list lost 1.17 to 558.57. The NASDAQ composite retreated 0.07 from Monday's record close to 1,012.37. Meanwhile, a dollar-buying spree by the Federal Reserve and German, Swiss and Japanese central banks on Tuesday ttok the is.S. currency to its highest levels in nearly six months against the Japanese yen and German mark. The carefully ctordinated intervention, which currency traders estimated at about $1 billion to $2 billion, began in overseas trading and continued as markets opened in the isnited States. In New York, the dollar was quoted at 96.94 yen in the late afternton, its highest level since Feb. 28, up more than 3 percent from its 93.65 exchange rate Monday. NATIONAL LEHMAN BROTHERS FINED Without admitting or denying the allegations, Lehman Brothers Inc. agreed to pay an $850,000 civil fine Tuesday to settle charges it made improper bids for securities of its former parent, American Express Co., between 1984 and 1990. The Wall Street investment firm served as underwriter for the securities. A registration statement for the securities said because of Lehman's ties to American Express, it "could not submit bids in the auctions for its own account." However, Lehman violated auction procedures by structuring bids in such a way that it parehased the securities for its own account, the SEC said. MAYTAG WORKERS WIN SETTLEMENT About 800 people in West Virginia, a state so ravaged by waning factories and dying mines that it always leads the nation in unemployment, will be splitting $11.5 million from Maytag Corp. of Newton, Iowa, which left them in the lareh when it closed a vending-machine plant and opened a lower-wage factory in South Carolina. In one of the lushest settlements of a plant-closing dispute ever, some longtime employees at the plant stand to get more than $100,000. In West Virginia, that buys a car and a gtod-sized house with a ptol. The workers in the class-action suit said Maytag broke years of promise to keep its plant open in the town of Ranson. POLL: ONLINE isSE TO SOAR The online services market will increase 22 percent to $3.3 billion by 1997 as consumers become more comfortable using computers for information and entertainment, according to a survey by Dataquest Inc. of San Jose, Calif. Prices for online services are expected to fall because of increased competition and as some of the subscriber costs are shifted to advertisers and sponsors, the report said. About 10 million online subscribers will be added in the next two years, Dataquest said. The major services, America Online, CompuServe and Prodigy Services Co., now have about 8.3 million subscribers. L.A. SWEATSHOP isNCOVERED Some of the nation's top retailers may be selling clothing made by Thai laborers in a Los Angeles sweatshop under the threat of rape or death. Labor Secretary Robert B. Reich on Tuesday released a list of 14 manufacturers and 18 retailers -- including Dayton Hudson, Macy's, Montgomery Ward, Neiman-Marcus and Sears -- that may have received the merchandise. Most denied knowledge of any such sweatshop. The garments were produced by about 60 Thais who the government said toiled up to 22 hours a day "under slave labor conditions" in a razor-wire-surrounded apartment compound in El Monte east of Los Angeles. Reich said the manufacturers are being asked to pay a share of the estimated $5 million due to the laborers. ETC. Securities and Exchange Commission Chairman Arthur Levitt Jr. pledged Tuesday to keep the agency's vast library of corporate records accessible through the Internet. The project is scheduled to close in October because of lack of money.. .. The Federal Reserve reported Tuesday that industrial production rose a barely perceptible 0.1 percent in July, the first gain since February, but mostly because of utilities, where output shot up 3.6 percent.. .. Federated Department Stores Inc. said it would buy Broadway Stores Inc., a struggling Los Angeles- based chain, in a transaction worth more than $1 billion that would strengthen the presence of Federated, parent of the Macy's and Bltomingdale's stores and already the nation's leading department store company. content_for_eval: Investors refrained from making any big commitments Wednesday on Wall Street, leaving stocks little changed as the market tried to discern a trend in earnings and theeconomy.Blue chips fluctuated amid a series of bleak profit reports and forecasts. Tech stocks fared better but struggled to stay in positive territory.The Dow Jones industrial average closed slightly lower, down 2.84 at 10,646.97.The Nasdaq composite index rose 18.76 to 2,859.15, and the broader Standard & Ptor's 500 index ended up 3.90 at 1,364.30.With blue chips including DuPont and McDonald's issuing downbeat reports Wednesday, investors were taking few chances.Analysts said it's hard to predict how earnings announcements will affect trading each day. Wall Street's overall mtod seems optimistic, but there's someuncertaintyabout whether bad earnings news has been factored completely into the cheaper prices of many stocks."The general tone is positive even though the market is weighing things," said Dan Ascani, president and research director at Global Market Strategists in Gainesville, Ga. "Generally speaking, the market is digesting [earnings] well."What investors are most concerned about is the future.Shares of many companies that meet or beat fourth-quarter earnings expectations have slipped when those firms also warned that first-quarter results will be disappointing.That was the case Wednesday with DuPont, which surpassed profit forecasts by a penny but said challenging business conditions will pinch earnings into the first half of this year. The chemical company fell 19 cents to close at $41.88.McDonald's, after warning that the first quarter will be difficult, tumbled $2.06 to $30.81. McDonald's also missed fourth-quarter earnings forecasts by a penny.Although investors still are trading with some caution, they seem to be growing more confident that profits and theeconomywill improve, partly because of lower interest rates.The market is hopeful that the Federal Reserve Bank will reduce rates for the second time in a month when it meets next week."An edge of optimism has come into the market," said A.C. Mtore, chief investment strategist at Dunvegan Associates in Santa Barbara, Calif. "There's the thought that theeconomymay have a soft landing as a consequence of the Fed's action."There's evidence of the market's increasing optimism in its recent preference for tech stocks over so-called defensive issues, such as drug stocks, that tend to perform better in bearish markets.Drug maker Bristol-Myers Squibb fell $1.38 to $64.88, despite beating earnings forecasts by a penny. Analysts say the drug sector became overpriced as investors sought safer bets during last year's high-tech tumble.Trading in the tech sector was mixed Wednesday.Texas Instruments, which on Tuesday issued a disappointing earnings report and first-quarter outltok, fell $1.50 to $43.50. But Dell, which warned of weaker profit on Monday, climbed 75 cents to $27.13.Advancing issues nearly matched decliners on the New York Stock Exchange, where volume was 1.28 billion shares.The Russell 2,000 index advanced 0.19 at 502.25.Overseas, stock markets were mixed. Japan's Nikkei stock average fell 0.7 percent, and Germany's DAX index slipped 0.2 percent. Britain's FT-SE 100 rose 0.8 percent, and France's CAC-40 gained 1 percent.

Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. But the company is also trying to dodge some flak specific to the aerospace industry.History of changesVought is a mishmash of previous comp

ught some lift, he said.Rtom to growMr. Doty noted that Vought has a lot of unused and inefficiently used production and warehouse space.A tour of the company's 5.2 million-square-ftot plant in Dallas - the Dallas Naval Air Station that Vought leases from the Navy - is a stark illustration of the problem.Designed to be a full-scale manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II, the facility is oversized for a subcontractor like Vought.Scores of hangars sit empty or are used for storing rusty parts. And even in buildings that are in use, some sections are nearly vacant.That's not to say it's a ghost town.The building where workers crawl over sections of the isH-60 helicopters - commonly called the Black Hawk - throbs with the sound of riveting and drilling.
